6: A direct revelation: he has been raised; he is not here. The resurrection announcement by the young man (angel) in the empty tomb is the counterpart to the Father's declaration at the baptism: the one identified as the beloved Son (Mark 1:11) is the one raised from the dead (Mark 16:6). Mark does not narrate resurrection appearances (in the original ending) but declares the resurrection and points forward to Galilee — the community is to follow the risen Lord as they followed the living Lord through Mark's Gospel. The resurrection vindicates everything Jesus claimed and fulfills every passion prediction; it is the Father's endorsement of the Son's entire Messianic-Servant mission.
1: A theme of faithful love expressed in the face of death: the women buy spices "so that they might go to anoint Jesus' body." They go to honor a dead man — they have no expectation of resurrection despite his three predictions of it (Mark 8:31, Mark 9:31, Mark 10:34). Their love does not require hope to act; they serve even in grief. The anointing intention echoes the woman at Bethany (Mark 14:8) who "anointed my body beforehand for burial" — the one who anointed him before the cross and the ones who come to anoint him after it are united in the same act of devoted service to the one whose death they mourn.
2: A revelation of the resurrection on the first day of the week — the dawn of the new creation: "very early on the first day of the week, just after sunrise." The timing is cosmic: the same phrase that begins Genesis 1 ("the beginning," "God said, 'Let there be light'") finds its echo in the dawn of the first day of the week when the Light of the world rises from the dead. The first day of the week becomes the Lord's Day for the church precisely because of what happens at this sunrise. The resurrection is the beginning of the new creation (2 Corinthians 5:17; Revelation 21:5).
3: A theme of human inability encountering divine action: "Who will roll the stone away from the entrance of the tomb?" — the women's question is the question of all who come to Christ with a need that exceeds their capacity to solve. The stone is too heavy; they cannot move it. They come anyway, asking the practical question on the road. The gospel consistently meets people at the point of their acknowledged inability: the stone that seals them from Christ has already been rolled away when they arrive. The obstacle they feared is gone before they get there.
4: A revelation of divine action preceding human arrival: "the stone, which was very large, had been rolled away." The rolling of the stone is not narrated in Mark (contrast Matthew 28:2); Mark simply records the discovery — the stone is already moved when the women arrive. The resurrection does not wait for human witnesses or human assistance. God acted in the darkness, while the women slept, before anyone came to observe. The resurrection is a divine act that human beings discover, not one they facilitate or witness in its occurrence. They arrive to find what has already happened.
5: A revelation of the angelic announcement at the empty tomb: "a young man dressed in a white robe sitting on the right side." The messenger in white at the right side — the position of honor and authority — is the divine attestation of the resurrection. The women's alarm (exethambēthēsan) is the appropriate response to an encounter with the numinous — the same reaction as at the transfiguration (Mark 9:6: ekphoboi). The empty tomb alone might be ambiguous (the body removed, an unknown explanation); the messenger clarifies what the empty tomb means: the crucified one is risen.
7: A direct revelation of Christ's forgiveness and restoration of Peter: "go, tell his disciples and Peter." Peter's specific naming in the resurrection announcement is the Good Shepherd coming after the one lost sheep of the inner circle. Peter had denied Christ three times (Mark 14:66-72) and "went outside and wept bitterly" (Luke 22:62). The resurrection's first pastoral act is to include by name the one who excluded himself by his failure. The risen Christ's first message is not condemnation of the denier but a specific summons. The "and Peter" is grace visible in two words.
8: A revelation of the resurrection as the mystery that exceeds immediate comprehension: "trembling and bewildered, the women went out and fled from the tomb. They said nothing to anyone, because they were afraid." The original ending of Mark does not conclude with triumphant proclamation but with fear and silence — the overwhelmed response to an encounter with the resurrection. The fear (ephobounto gar) is not unbelief but awe — the appropriate initial response to something that exceeds all categories. The gospel does not end here, because the reader now holds the news the women could not speak. The loop is open; the story continues in the one who reads it.
9: The Long Ending (Mark 16:9-20) is absent from the oldest Greek manuscripts (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) and most early witnesses; it is a second-century addition to provide a fuller conclusion. The tradition it contains — Mary Magdalene's appearance (Mark 16:9), the two disciples on the road (Mark 16:12), the commissioning (Mark 16:15-16), the ascension (Mark 16:19) — draws on authentic resurrection tradition attested in Luke and John. The Christological content is not invented; the question is only whether Mark wrote these verses. The risen Christ who appears, commissions, and ascends is the same one whose death and burial Mark has narrated.
10: A theme of the risen Christ's testimony reaching grieving disciples: Mary Magdalene goes and tells those "mourning and weeping" — the community of disciples in the aftermath of the crucifixion, grief-stricken and without hope. The resurrection testimony reaches the grief first: before any theological instruction, before any appearance to the group, the news of the risen Christ reaches those who mourn. The gospel is always good news to those in grief — the announcement that the one who was dead is alive is the answer to the mourning that death produces.
11: A revelation of the disciples' initial disbelief as the record of genuine historical testimony: "they did not believe it." The disciples' disbelief of Mary Magdalene's testimony is the NT's consistent account of the resurrection's initial reception (Luke 24:11: "their words seemed to them like nonsense"). The disbelief is not a failure of the witnesses but a confirmation of the testimony's credibility: these are not credulous enthusiasts who believe whatever they hope for. The resurrection overcame systematic skepticism — which is why the faith that results from it is faith in something that was confirmed against initial resistance.
12: A theme of the risen Christ appearing in unexpected form: "Jesus appeared in a different form to two of them while they were walking in the country" — corresponding to the Emmaus road (Luke 24:13-35). The unrecognized risen Christ walking with those who do not recognize him is the pattern of resurrection appearances: he is the same person and a different person, the same body and a transformed body. The resurrection body is not the resuscitation of a corpse but the transformation of the body that was buried. "We shall be like him, for we shall see him as he is" (1 John 3:2) — the transformation awaiting believers is the transformation already enacted in Christ.
13: A theme of the accumulation of resurrection testimony before belief breaks through: the two report to the rest; the rest do not believe them. Three testimony-chains fail before the appearance to the Eleven (Mark 16:14) breaks the pattern of disbelief. The accumulation of failed testimonies is historically significant: it means the resurrection faith was not adopted enthusiastically or credulously but was forced on people who resisted it. "He presented himself alive after his suffering by many convincing proofs" (Acts 1:3) — the proofs were necessary because the disciples' initial disposition was disbelief, not credulity.
14: A revelation of Christ confronting and overcoming the hardened hearts of his disciples with his own presence: "Later Jesus appeared to the Eleven as they were eating; he rebuked them for their lack of faith and their stubborn refusal to believe." The hardened heart (sklērokardia) diagnosed throughout Mark (Mark 6:52, Mark 8:17, Mark 10:5) is the final obstacle that the resurrection appearance itself overcomes. The rebuke is grace: he appears to them, he confronts their unbelief, he commissions them. The disciples who failed to understand the feedings, the transfiguration, and the passion predictions are now confronted with the risen Lord who will not leave them in their failure.
15: A direct revelation of the Great Commission as the risen Christ extending his own mission: "Go into all the world and preach the gospel to all creation." The mission that began in Galilee (Mark 1:14-15: "Jesus went into Galilee, proclaiming the good news of God... 'Repent and believe the good news!'") now extends to "all creation." The one who moved between Jewish and Gentile territory throughout his ministry (Mark 5:1, Mark 7:24, Mark 7:31) now commissions the movement to "all the world." The Gentile mission that was anticipated in the Gerasene demoniac (Mark 5:19-20) and the Syrophoenician woman (Mark 7:29) is now formally authorized by the risen Christ.
16: A direct revelation of salvation through faith in the risen Christ: "whoever believes and is baptized will be saved, but whoever does not believe will be condemned." The structure of salvation — believing the gospel and being baptized as its public expression — is the response the risen Christ calls for from the world he is sending his disciples to. The condemnation falls on unbelief (not on unbaptized status), because it is faith in the crucified and risen Christ that justifies (Romans 3:26). The one who died as a ransom for many (Mark 10:45) now calls the many to receive that ransom through faith.
17: A revelation of Christ's authority extending through his representatives by signs: "in my name they will drive out demons; they will speak in new tongues." The apostolic signs are extensions of Christ's own ministry (exorcism: Mark 1:25, Mark 3:11, Mark 5:8; new tongues: Acts 2:4). "In my name" is the authorization: the representatives act not by their own authority but by the authority of the one who sends them. Acts 3:6 illustrates this: "in the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, walk" — Peter acts as the agent of the absent-yet-present Christ. The signs authenticate the agents by the authority of the one whose name they invoke.
18: A theme of the supernatural protection of Christ's witnesses in hostile environments: "they will pick up snakes; when they drink deadly poison, it will not hurt them." Acts 28:3-6 records the fulfillment of the snake promise with Paul on Malta — the viper bite that produces no harm. The promise is not a license for recklessness but an assurance that the mission Christ sends his people on will not be frustrated by the dangers of the mission field. The healing by hand-laying (Acts 3:7, Mark 9:17, Mark 28:8) continues the same pattern: the hands that were extended on the cross extend healing through those who bear his name.
19: A direct fulfillment of Psalms 110:1: "after the Lord Jesus had spoken to them, he was taken up into heaven and he sat at the right hand of God." Psalms 110:1: "The LORD says to my lord: 'Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet.'" Jesus had cited this Psalm at his trial (Mark 14:62: "you will see the Son of Man sitting at the right hand of the Mighty One") as the Christological claim that the Sanhedrin called blasphemy. The Ascension and session at the right hand is the vindication of that claim. The one they condemned for claiming to sit at God's right hand now sits at God's right hand. The trial's verdict is reversed.
20: A direct revelation of the risen Christ continuing his mission through his church: "the Lord worked with them and confirmed his word by the signs that accompanied it." The missionary activity of Acts is the continuation of the Gospel of Mark: the disciples go out; the Lord works with them; the word is confirmed by signs. Mark's Gospel opens with "the beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ" (Mark 1:1) — and it ends without a true ending because the gospel does not end. The story Mark begins continues in Acts, in the church, in the proclamation of every generation, until the one who ascended to the Father's right hand returns.
